coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of orange fluorescence carbon dots with excitation independent emission for pH sensing and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Jiao, Yuan
Gong, Xiaojuan
Han, Hui
Gao, Yifang
Lu, Wenjing
Liu, Yang
Xian, Ming
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: High-efficient orange fluorescence nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were facilely prepared from P-phenylenediamine as a precursor via hydrothermal method. The as-prepared N-CDs with an average diameter of 3.65 nm displayed excitation-independent emission at 590 nm. The N-CDs demonstrated a remarkable fluorescence enhancement behavior with the increase of pH. A sigmoidal curve was well fitted using BiDoseResp equation with pKa1 3.57 and pKa2 6.01, which can be ascribed to the unique surface properties of N-CDs. Two-segment linear ranges of 2.6–4.6 and 5.0–6.8 broaden the response range to pH of the orange-emission N-CDs to some extent. The confocal fluorescent microscopic images of SMMC7721 cells were performed successfully, which demonstrating that N-CDs possess exceptional cell membrane permeability and can implement as biosensing platform to monitor pH fluctuations in living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
pH probe
Double pKa
Cellular imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and particle size distribution (top inset) of the as-synthesized N-CDs. (b) XPS survey scan of N-CDs. (c) C1s XPS, (d) N1s XPS and (e) O1s XPS of N-CDs. (f) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption, PL excitation (λex) and emission (λem) spectra of N-CDs. The inset displays photographs of the N-CDs under daylight (left) and UV irradiation (right) in aqueous solution. (b) PL spectra of N-CDs at different λex 440–560 nm. The concentration of N-CDs is 0.10 mg mL−1.

The PL spectra of 0.10 mg mL-1 N-CDs as pH decreases from 7.4 to 1.4 at λex of 566 nm. (b) The relationship between the PL intensity at λex/λem of 566/590 nm and pH. (c) Proposed sensing mechanism of N-CDs binding hydrogen ion.

Fluorescence intensity of 0.10 mg mL−1 N-CDs at different pH in the presence of various species: (1) blank, (2) K+, (3) Na+, (4) Ca2+, (5) Mg2+, (6) Ba2+, (7)Fe3+, (8) Cu2+, (9) Fe2+, (10) Al3+, (11) Zn2+, (12) Ni2+, (13) Mn2+, (14) Co2+, (15) Pb2+, (16) Cd2+, (17) Hg2+, (18) Ag+, (19) Sn2+, (20) Cr3+.

Changes in fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with time at pH 7.0, 4.8 and 2.0. Excitation and emission bandwidths are both set at 5 nm. (b) Changes in the PL intensity of N-CDs cycled between pH 7.4 and 4.7 λex/λem are 566/590 nm.

Confocal fluorescence images of 0.6 mg mL-1 N-CDs in SMMC7721 cells at pH (A) 7.4, (D) 4.8 and (G) 2.0. (B) Bright field image, and (C) merged image of A and B at pH 7.4. (E) Bright field image, and (F) merged image of (D) and (E) at pH 4.7. (H) Bright field image, and (I) merged image of (G) and (H) at pH 1.4.
Illustration of the synthetic procedure of the N-CDs and the N-CDs based fluorescence biosensing platform for the detection of pH in living cells.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of orange fluorescence carbon dots with excitation independent emission for pH sensing and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Washington State University, Pullman, WA, 99164, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-efficient orange fluorescence nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were facilely prepared from P-phenylenediamine as a precursor via hydrothermal method. The as-prepared N-CDs with an average diameter of 3.65 nm displayed excitation-independent emission at 590 nm. The N-CDs demonstrated a remarkable fluorescence enhancement behavior with the increase of pH. A sigmoidal curve was well fitted using BiDoseResp equation with pKa1 3.57 and pKa2 6.01, which can be ascribed to the unique surface properties of N-CDs. Two-segment linear ranges of 2.6–4.6 and 5.0–6.8 broaden the response range to pH of the orange-emission N-CDs to some extent. The confocal fluorescent microscopic images of SMMC7721 cells were performed successfully, which demonstrating that N-CDs possess exceptional cell membrane permeability and can implement as biosensing platform to monitor pH fluctuations in living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attention owing to their excellent optical properties [1], high fluorescent stability, good biocompatibility and high resistance to photobleaching [2–5]. All these exceptional characteristics of CDs have been utilized advantageously in fluorescent probes [6–10], bioimaging [11–15] and drug delivery [16–19] etc. In most of applications, a variety of CDs frequently emitted blue or green light accompanied by excitation dependent emission [20–22], which greatly restricted their applications in biology on account of their weak penetration capability and severe photo damage to biological tissues [23,24]. Meanwhile, the excitation dependent emission of these blue or green emission CDs was frequently ascribed to the different photon absorption mechanisms of the CDs and they often delivered very low QYs for yellow and red lights [24–26]. Thus, the development of CDs with single long-wavelength emission becomes extremely critical to extend the utilization of CDs.

Currently, some attempts have been made toward long-wavelength fluorescent CDs together with excitation independent emission. For example, Jiang et al. prepared blue, green, and red emissions CDs using ethanol as reaction solvent and demonstrated their unique photoluminescence features in multiplexed cellular imaging [27]. Wang et al. synthesized the strong solvent-dependent emission CDs using diphenyl ether as a reaction medium and embedded it in solid media to form CDs films as solid phosphors [28]. Chen et al. reported the solvothermal synthesis of organic-soluble CDs in formamide organic media and demonstrated their utilization in LEDs [23]. Such kinds of long-wavelength emissions CDs possess relatively weak solubility in water with the organic media as the reaction solvent, which presented tremendous limitation in biosensing and bioimaging [29,30]. Thus, the development of high-efficient long-emitting CDs with good water solubility along with their fluorescent based biosensing applications are still of important concern.

Herein, we fabricated orange emissive carbon dots N-CDs through simple hydrothermal method using P-phenylenediamine as the precursor and water as the reaction solvent, respectively. The introduction of trace hydrochloric acid promotes the dissolution of P-phenylenediamine in water. The prepared N-CDs with excellent water-solubility displayed excitation wavelength independent and pH-dependent behavior. When the pH is increased from 1.4 to 7.4, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs presents a remarkable enhancement. The pKa value of N-CDs was proved to be 3.57 and 6.01, which were corresponding to the pH linearity range of 2.6–4.6 and 5.0–6.8, respectively. The N-CDs also shared favorable biocompatibility, excellent reversibility, ultra-low toxicity and cellular labeling capability etc. Holding these superior properties, the N-CDs was implemented as a biosensing platform to monitor physiological pH fluctuations in living cells. The N-CDs can be regarded as excellent biosensors for real-time monitoring of the intracellular pH under physiological conditions in the disease diagnosis, biosensing and biomedical fields (See Scheme 1 ).

P-phenylenediamine, sodium chloride (NaCl), tetrahydrofuran (THF) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Concentrated phosphoric acid (H3PO4) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were from Beijing Chemical Works. The 0.02 mol L−1 phosphoric acid solution was titrated with NaOH (1.0 mol L−1) and H3PO4 (1.0 mol L−1) to prepare phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solutions with different pH values. The solutions of metal ions were prepared from chloride and nitrate salts of blank K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Cr3+. All aqueous solutions were prepared with distilled deionized water (≥18.25 MΩ cm) from a Milli-Q Plus system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

216 mg of P-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 20 mL of water and adjusted the pH value to 3–4 by adding 300 μL HCl. Then the mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the obtained deep purple-red suspensions was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15min and then dialyzed in a 1 L DDI water through a dialysis membrane with an MWCO of 500–1000 Da with stirring and replenishing with fresh DDI water every 24 h for 3 days. The final product was freeze-dried to afford a powder and the yield of the synthesized N-CDs was 90.3% (Fig. S1).

The pH was measured at various pH in 0.02 mol L−1 phosphate buffered saline. Typically, 200 μL, 1.0 mg mL−1 of N-CDs solution was added to 2.0 mL of PBS with different pH values. The fluorescence spectroscopy was employed after 2 min. Measurements were performed in triplicate.

According to the literature [31–33], human Hepatic carcinoma SMMC7721 cells were seeded into Costar@ 96-well cell culture cluster with a density of 4 × 103 cells/well in 100 μL of Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) medium with 10% FBS at 37 °C with 5.0% CO2 atmosphere. After the original medium was removed, the SMMC7721 cells were incubated with different concentration N-CDs (10–50 μg mL−1) for 48 h. At least five parallel samples were performed in each group. The cells in a culture medium and without N-CDs were served as the control. The cells were washed by PBS for three times, then added 20 μL MTT (5.0 mg mL−1 PBS) reagent to each well with fresh culture medium. After 4 h incubation, MTT-containing culture medium was removed from the wells and 150 μL of DMSO was added per well to dissolve the intracellular blue-violet formazan crystals. Finally, the SunRise microplate reader (Tecan Austria GmbH, GrÖdig, Austria) was used to record the optical density (OD) of each sample at a wavelength of 566 nm. The cell viability was calculated as following equation:
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
(
%
)
=
O D
T r e a t e d
O D
C o n t r o l
×
100 % ,
where OD Treated and OD Control were obtained in the presence and absence of N-CDs, respectively.

Human hepatic carcinoma SMMC7721 cells were cultured in DMEM medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and inoculated at 37 °C under 5.0% CO2 for 48 h then removed the medium and washed cells with PBS (pH = 7.4) for three times. The N-CDs solution using ultrapure water as a solvent were added to different pH PBS buffers and incubated with the cells for another 30 min at 37 °C. The concentration of N-CDs was kept at 0.60 mg mL−1. Afterwards, the cells were washed again using different pH PBS buffers for three times to remove residual N-CDs. The cellular imaging was performed using confocal laser scanning microscopy.

The morphology of N-CDs was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the N-CDs are well dispersed with an average diameter of 3.65 ± 0.3 nm. The size distribution of the N-CDs is preferably Gaussian and half full width of the fitting curve is 0.9 nm, demonstrating that the N-CDs possess the narrow size distribution. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed as a powerful tool for investigated surface functional groups and chemical compositions of the N-CDs. The FTIR spectrum was shown in Fig. 1f. The absorption peaks at 3415 cm−1 and 3330 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of -OH/N-H. There are also stretching and bending vibrations of -C-H at around 3029, 2977and 2883 cm−1. The absorption bands at 1632 cm−1 and 1571 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of C=N and C=C [34]. Above analysis shown that the N-CDs is composed of aromatic structures.

According to the survey XPS scan of the N-CDs, the full spectra presented in Fig. 1b show three typical peaks at 285 eV, 400 eV and 531 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1sand O1s, respectively [35–37]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s was shown in Fig. 1c, there existed three peaks representing sp2 carbon (C=C, 284.7 eV), sp3 carbon (C−O/C−N, 286.0 eV) and carboxyl carbon (COOH, 288.8 eV). As shown in Fig. 1d, N1s could be resolved into two peaks of 397.9 eV and 399.1 eV, indicating the presence of pyridine-N and pyrrole-N structures. The O1s XPS spectra in Fig. 1e exhibits two peaks at 531.2 eV and 532.9 eV corresponding to C-OH/C-O-C and -C=O.

The optical properties of N-CDs were verified by UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra. As shown in black line in Fig. 2 a, the obvious absorption peaks of N-CDs presented at 234 nm and 281 nm could be ascribed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 and the n→π* transition of the C-N bond. A weak broad absorption is exhibited from 450 nm to 570 nm, representing surface absorption of the N-CDs and its subsequent excitation of the fluorescence emission. The blue and red lines in Fig. 2a demonstrated that the optimal excitation (λex) and emission (λem) wavelengths of N-CDs are located at 566 nm and 590 nm. Under irradiation of 365 nm UV light, the N-CDs aqueous solution can immediately exhibit strong orange photoluminescence (Fig. 2a inset).

The optical properties of N-CDs were further explored and the excitation-independent behavior was studied and described in Fig. 2b. When the excitation wavelength is changed from 440 nm to 560 nm, all the emission is located at 590 nm. This behavior is contributed to the π→π* transition of graphitic structure of the carbon cores. As shown in TEM, the N-CDs exhibits fewer surface layers with a uniform crystalline core, which is likely to result in the excitation wavelength independence of N-CDs. Excitation dependent emission is considered to be a general property of CDs for multicolor applications, but complex emission spectra are difficult to distinguish from one another in practical applications. Thus, the CDs with single emission are still highly desirable. Using Rhodamine B as a reference (QY = 89% in ethanol, λex = 495 nm), the relative quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was measured to be 11.5% at λex/λem of 495/515–750 nm (Fig. S2).

As pH plays pivotal roles in various cellular behaviors and pathological processes, the detection of pH is of great importance. The pH-dependent behavior of the N-CDs was investigated in Fig. S3, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is significantly reduced both in acidic and alkaline environments. In addition, the fluorescence peak has a slight red shift with the decreasing of pH. The N-CDs PL spectra variation over a wide pH range of 1.4–7.4 under λex of 566 nm was depicted in Fig. 3 a. The N-CDs demonstrated a remarkable fluorescence enhancement behavior with the increase of pH in the range of 2–7. A sigmoidal curve was well fitted using BiDoseResp equation:
y = 3077.07 + 265643
[
0.5
1 + 10
( 3.57 − x )
+
0.5
1 + 10
( 6.01 − X )
1.26
]
,
R
2
= 0.999 .

The N-CDs can achieve detection of near-neutral pH since the normal physiological hydrogen ion concentration varies in narrow range [38,39]. The fluorescence intensity shows good linear response to pH in the range of 2.6–4.6 and 5.0–6.8. The linear regression equation are (F0-F)/F0 = 1.538–0.227 pH, R2 = 0.993, RSD = 1.27%; (F0-F)/F0 = 1.854–0.271 pH, R2 = 0.988, RSD = 2.01%, where F0 and F are the PL intensities of the N-CDs at pH 7.0 and other pH, respectively. All this results demonstrate that the N-CDs can monitor pH quantitatively in living cells within the near neutrality range.

The N-CDs display a remarkable response to pH with double PKa and the proposed sensing mechanism of N-CDs binding hydrogen ions is described in Fig. 3c. According to the N1s spectrum of N-CDs, there are many nitrogen atoms bonded to the edge of the sp2-hybridized carbon core in the form of pyridine and pyrrole, which pKa are 5.25 and 17.5, respectively [40]. The basicity of pyrrolic is relatively weaker than pyridinic because the lone pair of electrons on the pyrrolic nitrogen was involved in p-π conjugation. In the experiments, the nitrogen atoms on the pyridine ring are protonated firstly and shown the linearity range of 5.0–6.8; As the decreases of pH, the pyrrole nitrogen is protonated and exhibits the linearity range of 2.6–4.6 [41]. This unique structure allows N-CDs to exhibit double PKa values during PH detection.

The selectivity response of N-CDs to H+ over representative metal ions was studied at pH 7.0, 4.8 and 2.0, respectively (Fig. 4 ). After interactions with different metal ions at a concentration of 10 mM (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+,Al3+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Cr3+), the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs has no obvious emission change at different pH. The result reveals that N-CDs exhibits high selectivity toward hydrogen ions over competitive metal ions.

The effects of ionic strength (expressed as NaCl concentration) on the fluorescence stability of N-CDs was investigated in Fig. S4. In the presence of different concentrations of NaCl, the PL intensity of N-CDs did not change much, which is beneficial to the practical application of N-CDs under physical salt conditions. The fluorescence decay of N-CDs at different pH (pH = 3–7) was measured in Fig. S5, the time-correlated single photon counting method at 590 nm represents a bi-exponential fitting curve, and the average lifetime is calculated to be 1.97 ns, 2.43 ns, 2.53 ns, 2.59 ns and 2.71 ns, respectively. The lifetime of N-CDs has slightly increased with the increment of pH in the range of 3–7, but there is no obvious difference.

The stability of the N-CDs at different pH (pH = 3.0, 4.8 and 7.0) was verified by measuring the fluorescence response for 3 h (Fig. 5 a). The PL intensity was monitored continuously at λex/em = 566/590 nm. In addition, the photostability of N-CDs for seven day was investigated and showed in Fig. S6. These results demonstrated that the N-CDs possess excellent photostability and can be utilized to monitor the PL changes in real time. Fig. 5b shows the reversibility of N-CDs to H+. The pH of the aqueous solution was adjusted between 7.4 and 2.0 using concentrated hydrochloric acid and aqueous sodium hydroxide [42–44]. After several cycles, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs successfully recovered in near neutral conditions. Owing to many lipids existed in the cells, it is of great importance to examine the fluorescence change of N-CDs in THF/H2O mixture. As presented in Fig. S8, the fluorescence of N-CDs have no obvious variation at lower levels of THF and it begins to decrease only when the content of THF in the mixed system reaches a higher level. The result demonstrated that N-CDs can be utilized in biological system.

Standard MTT assays were performed using Hepatic carcinoma SMMC7721 cells to assess the cytotoxicity of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. S7, over 85% of cells are viable after incubation of SMMC7721 cells with either of the N-CDs at concentrations ranging from 10 to 50 μg mL−1 for 24 h, demonstrating low toxicity of the N-CDs and inferring their potential use in cellular imaging.

The SMMC7721 cells were selected as model organisms for assessing the potential of N-CDs in vitro imaging at pH 7.4, 4.8 and 2.0. The orange fluorescence (575–750 nm) is captured by 514 nm laser. As shown in Fig. 6 a, SMMC7721 cells showed bright orange fluorescence emission at pH 7.4 clearly; Accompanied the extracellular pH dropped to 4.8, the orange fluorescence intensity also decreased (Fig. 6b); The fluorescence intensity of SMMC7721 cells is hardly found when the pH reduces to 2.0 (Fig. 6c) [45]. The results indicate that the as-prepared N-CDs can easily penetrate the cell membrane into the cells and monitor pH fluctuations in live cells.

In conclusion, we present a simple one−step hydrothermal carbonization method to synthesize nitrogen-containing CDs with P-phenylenediamine as the precursor. The as-synthesized N-CDs exhibitorange emission with an excitation wavelength independent property. It is noted that the N-CDs has excellent dispersion in aqueous solutions and relatively narrow in size distribution. The N-CDs also displays a remarkable response to pH with double pKa, which can be explained as the different basicity of the nitrogen-containing functional groups existed in the structure. It is also expected that the N-CDs can be regarded as a promising material for constructing practical fluorescent nanosensors for real-time tracking of the intracellular pH in physiological system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705101, 21475080 and 21575084) and Shanxi Province Hundred Talents Project.
